items.title: Publishing AI models to Docker Hub
items.author: Kevin Wittek
items.content:
When we first released Docker Model Runner, it came with built-in support for running AI models published and maintained by Docker on Docker Hub. This made it simple to pull a model like or and start using it locally with familiar Docker-style commands.

Model Runner now supports three new commands: , , and . These enable you to share models with your team, your organization, or the wider community. Whether you’re managing your own fine-tuned models or curating a set of open-source models, Model Runner now lets you publish them to Docker Hub or any other OCI Artifact compatible Container Registry.  For teams using Docker Hub, enterprise features like Registry Access Management (RAM) provide policy-based controls and guardrails to help enforce secure, consistent access.

Tagging and pushing to Docker Hub

Let’s start by republishing an existing model from Docker Hub under your own namespace.

# Step 1: Pull the model from Docker Hub
$ docker model pull ai/smollm2

# Step 2: Tag it for your own organization
$ docker model tag ai/smollm2 myorg/smollm2

# Step 3: Push it to Docker Hub
$ docker model push myorg/smollm2

That’s it! Your model is now available at and ready to be consumed using Model Runner by anyone with access.

Pushing to other container registries

Model Runner supports other container registries beyond Docker Hub, including GitHub Container Registry (GHCR).

# Step 1: Tag for GHCR
$ docker model tag ai/smollm2 ghcr.io/myorg/smollm2

# Step 2: Push to GHCR
$ docker model push ghcr.io/myorg/smollm2

Authentication and permissions work just like they do with regular Docker images in the context of GHCR, so you can leverage your existing workflow for managing registry credentials.

Packaging a custom GGUF file

Want to publish your own model file? You can use the package command to wrap a .file into a Docker-compatible OCI artifact and directly push it into a Container Registry, such as Docker Hub.

# Step 1: Download a model, e.g. from HuggingFace
$ curl -L -o model.gguf https://huggingface.co/TheBloke/Mistral-7B-v0.1-GGUF/resolve/main/mistral-7b-v0.1.Q4_K_M.gguf

# Step 2: Package and push it
$ docker model package --gguf "$(pwd)/model.gguf" --push myorg/mistral-7b-v0.1:Q4_K_M

You’ve now turned a raw model file in GGUF format into a portable, versioned, and sharable artifact that works seamlessly with .

We’ve seen how easy it is to publish your own models using Docker Model Runner’s new tag, push, and package commands. These additions bring the familiar Docker developer experience to the world of AI model sharing. Teams and enterprises using Docker Hub can securely manage access and control for their models, just like with container images, making it easier to scale GenAI applications across teams.

items.title: How to Build, Run, and Package AI Models Locally with Docker Model Runner
items.author: Vladimir Mikhalev
items.content:
As a Senior DevOps Engineer and Docker Captain, I’ve helped build AI systems for everything from retail personalization to medical imaging. One truth stands out: AI capabilities are core to modern infrastructure.

This guide will show you how to run and package local AI models with Docker Model Runner — a lightweight, developer-friendly tool for working with AI models pulled from Docker Hub or Hugging Face. You’ll learn how to run models in the CLI or via API, publish your own model artifacts, and do it all without setting up Python environments or web servers.

What is AI in Development?

Artificial Intelligence (AI) refers to systems that mimic human intelligence, including:

  • Making decisions via machine learning
  • Understanding language through NLP
  • Recognizing images with computer vision
  • Learning from new data automatically

Common Types of AI in Development:

  • : Learns from structured and unstructured data
  • : Neural networks for pattern recognition
  • Natural Language Processing (NLP): Understands/generates human language
  • : Recognizes and interprets images

Why Package and Run Your Own AI Model?

Local model packaging and execution offer full control over your AI workflows. Instead of relying on external APIs, you can run models directly on your machine — unlocking:

  • Faster inference with local compute (no latency from API calls)
  • Greater privacy by keeping data and prompts on your own hardware
  • Customization through packaging and versioning your own models
  • Seamless CI/CD integration with tools like Docker and GitHub Actions
  • Offline capabilities for edge use cases or constrained environments

Platforms like Docker and Hugging Face make cutting-edge AI models instantly accessible without building from scratch. Running them locally means lower latency, better privacy, and faster iteration.

Real-World Use Cases for AI

  • Chatbots & Virtual Assistants: Automate support (e.g., ChatGPT, Alexa)
  • : Create text, art, music (e.g., Midjourney, Lensa)
  • : Autocomplete and debug code (e.g., GitHub Copilot)
  • : Recommend products based on behavior
  • : Analyze scans for faster diagnosis

How to Package and Run AI Models Locally with Docker Model Runner

Step 0 — Enable Docker Model Runner

Go to Settings → Features in development

Under the Experimental features tab, enable Access experimental features

Quit and reopen Docker Desktop to ensure changes take effect

Reopen Settings → Features in development

Switch to the Beta tab and check Enable Docker Model Runner

Enable host-side TCP support to access the API from localhost

Once enabled, you can use the CLI and manage models in the Models tab.

Screenshot of Docker Desktop’s Features in development tab with Docker Model Runner and Dev Environments enabled.

docker model pull ai/smollm2

Or from Hugging Face (GGUF format):

docker model pull hf.co/bartowski/Llama-3.2-1B-Instruct-GGUF

Only are supported. GGUF (GPT-style General Use Format) is a lightweight binary file format designed for efficient local inference, especially with CPU-optimized runtimes like llama.cpp. It includes the model weights, tokenizer, and metadata all in one place, making it ideal for packaging and distributing LLMs in containerized environments.

Step 2: Tag and Push to Local Registry (Optional)

If you want to push models to a private or local registry:

Tag model with your registry’s address:

docker model tag hf.co/bartowski/Llama-3.2-1B-Instruct-GGUF localhost:5000/foobar

Run a local Docker registry:

docker run -d -p 6000:5000 --name registry registry:2

Push the model to the local registry:

docker model push localhost:6000/foobar

Check your local models with:

docker model run ai/smollm2 "What is Docker?"

docker model run ai/smollm2

Models are loaded into memory on demand and unloaded after 5 minutes of inactivity.

Step 4: Test via OpenAI-Compatible API

To call the model from the host:

  1. Enable for Model Runner (via Docker Desktop GUI or CLI)

Screenshot of Docker Desktop’s Features in development tab showing host-side TCP support enabled for Docker Model Runner.

docker desktop enable model-runner --tcp 12434
  1. Send a prompt using the OpenAI-compatible chat endpoint:
curl http://localhost:12434/engines/llama.cpp/v1/chat/completions \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -d '{
    "model": "ai/smollm2",
    "messages": [
      {"role": "system", "content": "You are a helpful assistant."},
      {"role": "user", "content": "Tell me about the fall of Rome."}
    ]
  }'

No API key required — this runs locally and securely on your machine.

Step 5: Package Your Own Model

You can package your own as a Docker-compatible artifact if you already have a file — such as one downloaded from Hugging Face or converted using tools like .

This guide assumes you already have a model file. It does not cover how to train or convert models to GGUF.

docker model package \
  --gguf "$(pwd)/model.gguf" \
  --license "$(pwd)/LICENSE.txt" \
  --push registry.example.com/ai/custom-llm:v1

This is ideal for custom-trained or private models. You can now pull it like any other model:

docker model pull registry.example.com/ai/custom-llm:v1

Step 6: Optimize & Iterate

  • Use to monitor model usage and debug issues
  • Set up CI/CD to automate pulls, scans, and packaging
  • Track model lineage and training versions to ensure consistency
  • Use semantic versioning (, etc.) instead of when packaging custom models
  • Only one model can be loaded at a time; requesting a new model will unload the previous one.

Compose Integration (Optional)

Docker Compose v2.35+ (included in Docker Desktop 4.41+) introduces support for AI model services using a new . You can define models directly in your and reference them in app services using .

During , Docker Model Runner automatically pulls the model and starts it on the host system, then injects connection details into dependent services using environment variables such as and , where matches the name of the model service.

This enables seamless multi-container AI applications — with zero extra glue code. Learn more.

Navigating AI Development Challenges

  • : Use quantized GGUF models
  • : Never run unknown models; validate sources and attach licenses
  • : Mask PII, respect data consent
  • : Run locally to avoid cloud compute bills
  • Prefer GGUF models for optimal CPU inference
  • Use the flag when packaging custom models to ensure compliance
  • Use versioned tags (e.g., ) instead of
  • Monitor model logs using
  • Validate model sources before pulling or packaging
  • Only pull models from trusted sources (e.g., Docker Hub’s ai/ namespace or verified Hugging Face repos).
  • Review the license and usage terms for each model before packaging or deploying.
  • Support for Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG)
  • Expanded multimodal support (text + images, video, audio)
  • LLMs as services in Docker Compose (Requires Docker Compose v2.35+)
  • More granular Model Dashboard features in Docker Desktop
  • Secure packaging and deployment pipelines for private AI models

Docker Model Runner lets DevOps teams treat models like any other artifact — pulled, tagged, versioned, tested, and deployed.

You don’t need a GPU cluster or external API to build AI apps. Learn more and explore everything you can do with Docker Model Runner:

  • Pull prebuilt models from Docker Hub or Hugging Face
  • Run them locally using the CLI, API, or Docker Desktop’s Model tab
  • Package and push your own models as OCI artifacts
  • Integrate with your CI/CD pipelines securely

You can also find other helpful information to get started at: 

You’re not just deploying containers — you’re delivering intelligence.

items.title: Deep Dive: Automating Kubernetes User Access with Terraform & Bash
items.author: Manohar Shetty
items.content:
🚀 Deep Dive: Automating Kubernetes Kind cluster — User Access with Terraform & Bash

In the DevOps world, automation isn’t just a convenience — it’s a necessity. From infrastructure provisioning to managing secure user access, repeatable and auditable workflows are key. In this blog, we’ll walk through two essential automation pieces:

  • 📦 Provisioning Kubernetes infrastructure using Terraform
  • 🔐 Creating secure, namespace-scoped Kubernetes users with a Bash script

🌐 Part 1: Infrastructure Provisioning with Terraform

Before we can manage users, we need a Kubernetes cluster to work with. Here’s a brief overview of how Terraform helps provision this infrastructure.

provider "aws" {  region = "ap-south-1"
resource "aws_vpc" "prod_vpc" {
  cidr_block = "10.0.0.0/16"    Name = "prod-VPC"}
data "aws_availability_zones" "available" {}
resource "aws_subnet" "prod_subnet" {
  vpc_id                  = aws_vpc.prod_vpc.id
  cidr_block              = "10.0.1.0/24"
  map_public_ip_on_launch = true
  availability_zone       = data.aws_availability_zones.available.names[0]
    Name = "prod-Public-1"}
resource "aws_internet_gateway" "prod_igw" {
  vpc_id = aws_vpc.prod_vpc.id    Name = "prod-IGW"}
resource "aws_route_table" "prod_rt" {
  vpc_id = aws_vpc.prod_vpc.id    Name = "prod-Public-RT"}
resource "aws_route" "internet_access" {
  route_table_id         = aws_route_table.prod_rt.id
  destination_cidr_block = "0.0.0.0/0"
  gateway_id             = aws_internet_gateway.prod_igw.id
resource "aws_route_table_association" "prod_subnet_assoc" {
  subnet_id      = aws_subnet.prod_subnet.id
  route_table_id = aws_route_table.prod_rt.id
}
resource "aws_security_group" "prod_sg" {
  vpc_id = aws_vpc.prod_vpc.id    from_port   = 0    protocol    = "-1"
    cidr_blocks = ["0.0.0.0/0"]
  }    from_port   = 0    protocol    = "-1"
    cidr_blocks = ["0.0.0.0/0"]
  }    Name = "prod-SG"}
resource "aws_instance" "mysql-server" {
  ami                    = "ami-03bb6d83c60fc5f7c"
  instance_type          = "t2.medium"
  key_name               = "testing-dev-1"
  subnet_id              = aws_subnet.prod_subnet.id
  vpc_security_group_ids = [aws_security_group.prod_sg.id]
  user_data              = file("user_banner.sh")
  # # Set root volume size to 25GB
  # root_block_device {
  #   volume_size           = 25
  #   volume_type           = "gp3"
  #   delete_on_termination = true
  # }    Name = "mysql-server"}
# resource "aws_instance" "app-server" {
#   ami                    = "ami-03bb6d83c60fc5f7c"
#   instance_type          = "t2.medium"
#   key_name               = "testing-dev-1"
#   subnet_id              = aws_subnet.prod_subnet.id
#   vpc_security_group_ids = [aws_security_group.prod_sg.id]
#   user_data              = file("user_banner.sh")
#     Name = "app-server"# }output "mysql_server_public_ip" {
  value = aws_instance.mysql-server.public_ip
}
#output "app_server_public_ip" {
# value = aws_instance.app-server.public_ip

This sets up the backend and downloads necessary provider plugins.

2. View the Execution Plan

Generates a plan of what Terraform will create or modify.

Creates your AWS EC2 instances and other resources.

  • A set of EC2 instances (kind cluster)

Once server is created. Install docker & kubectl on the server

sudo apt install docker.io -y

Step2: Install Kubectl & Kind Binaries

sudo apt update -y && sudo apt upgrade -ysnap install kubectl --classic
curl -Lo ./kind https://kind.sigs.k8s.io/dl/v0.22.0/kind-linux-amd64
chmod +x ./kind
sudo mv ./kind /usr/local/bin/

3. Create a configuration file

vi kind-configkind: Cluster
apiVersion: kind.x-k8s.io/v1alpha4
nodes:  extraPortMappings:    hostPort: 30000    hostPort: 30001- role: worker
kind create cluster --name=multi-node-cluster --config=kind-config
kubectl get nodesNAME                                STATUS  ROLES            AGE  VERSION
multi-node-cluster-control-plane    Ready    control-plane   2m   v1.32.6
multi-node-cluster-worker           Ready    worker          2m   v1.32.6
multi-node-cluster-worker           Ready    worker          2m   v1.32.6

Configuring Email Delivery on Kubernetes

  • sudo apt update -y: Refreshes the package index.
  • sudo apt install msmtp mpack ca-certificates: Installs the SMTP client and attachments utility with SSL support.
  • sudo apt install mutt -y: Installs the mail client to help format and send the message.
  • sudo ln -sf /usr/bin/msmtp /usr/sbin/sendmail: Creates a symlink so that commands expecting sendmail use msmtp under the hood.
sudo apt update -ysudo apt install msmtp mpack ca-certificatessudo ln -sf /usr/bin/msmtp /usr/sbin/sendmailauth ontls_trust_file /etc/ssl/certs/ca-certificates.crthost smtp.gmail.comfrom tradingcontentdrive@gmail.com
user tradingcontentdrive@gmail.com
password <your app password>mpack -s "Your Kubeconfig File" /root/.kube/config manohar.shetty507@gmail.com

This script automates the creation of a Kubernetes user with scoped access inside a Kind (Kubernetes in Docker) cluster, generates the necessary TLS certificates, creates a kubeconfig file, configures RBAC roles, and finally emails the kubeconfig to the user. Let’s break down every part.

#!/bin/bashset -euo pipefail
#----------------------------#
# 🚀 Kubernetes User Creator #
#----------------------------#
USER="$1"EMAIL="$3"KIND_CONTAINER="multi-node-cluster-control-plane"GREEN='\033[0;32m'BLUE='\033[1;34m'NC='\033[0m' # No Colorif [[ -z "$USER" || -z "$NAMESPACE" || -z "$EMAIL" ]]; then
  echo -e "${RED}❗ Usage: $0 <username> <namespace> <email>${NC}"
  exit 1
echo -e "${BLUE}👤 Creating user '${YELLOW}$USER${BLUE}' in namespace '${YELLOW}$NAMESPACE${BLUE}'...${NC}"
# 🔍 Check if Kind control plane is running
if ! docker ps --format '{{.Names}}' | grep -q "^${KIND_CONTAINER}$"; then
  echo -e "${RED}❌ Kind container '$KIND_CONTAINER' is not running!${NC}"
  exit 1
# 🔐 Generate keys and CSR
echo -e "${GREEN}🔑 Generating key and CSR for ${USER}...${NC}"
openssl genrsa -out "$OUTPUT_DIR/$USER.key" 2048
openssl req -new -key "$OUTPUT_DIR/$USER.key" -out "$OUTPUT_DIR/$USER.csr" -subj "/CN=$USER/O=$USER"
# 📜 Sign CSR using Kind's CA
echo -e "${GREEN}📜 Signing CSR inside Kind container...${NC}"
docker cp "$OUTPUT_DIR/$USER.csr" "$KIND_CONTAINER:/var/tmp/$USER.csr"
docker exec "$KIND_CONTAINER" openssl x509 -req -in "/var/tmp/$USER.csr" \
  -CA /etc/kubernetes/pki/ca.crt \
  -CAkey /etc/kubernetes/pki/ca.key \  -out "/var/tmp/$USER.crt" -days 365
docker cp "$KIND_CONTAINER:/var/tmp/$USER.crt" "$OUTPUT_DIR/$USER.crt"
echo -e "${GREEN}⚙️  Building kubeconfig for ${USER}...${NC}"
CLUSTER_NAME=$(kubectl config view --minify -o jsonpath='{.clusters[0].name}')
CLUSTER_SERVER=$(kubectl config view --minify -o jsonpath='{.clusters[0].cluster.server}')
kubectl config view --raw --minify -o jsonpath='{.clusters[0].cluster.certificate-authority-data}' | base64 -d > "$OUTPUT_DIR/ca.crt"
KUBECONFIG_FILE="$OUTPUT_DIR/$USER.kubeconfig"
kubectl config --kubeconfig="$KUBECONFIG_FILE" set-cluster "$CLUSTER_NAME" \
  --server="$CLUSTER_SERVER" \
  --certificate-authority="$OUTPUT_DIR/ca.crt" \
  --embed-certs=true
kubectl config --kubeconfig="$KUBECONFIG_FILE" set-credentials "$USER" \
  --client-certificate="$OUTPUT_DIR/$USER.crt" \
  --client-key="$OUTPUT_DIR/$USER.key" \
  --embed-certs=true
kubectl config --kubeconfig="$KUBECONFIG_FILE" set-context "$USER-context" \
  --cluster="$CLUSTER_NAME" \  --namespace="$NAMESPACE"
kubectl config --kubeconfig="$KUBECONFIG_FILE" use-context "$USER-context"
# 🛡️ Create RBAC resources
echo -e "${GREEN}🔐 Creating RBAC roles and bindings...${NC}"
cat <<EOF | kubectl apply -f -
apiVersion: rbac.authorization.k8s.io/v1metadata:  name: ${USER}-dev-role- apiGroups: ["", "apps", "batch"]
  resources: ["pods", "deployments", "jobs", "services", "configmaps", "secrets"]
  verbs: ["get", "watch", "list", "create", "update", "patch", "delete"]
kubectl create rolebinding "${USER}-dev-binding" \
  --namespace="$NAMESPACE" \
  --role="${USER}-dev-role" \
  --user="$USER" \
  --dry-run=client -o yaml | kubectl apply -f -
chmod 600 "$KUBECONFIG_FILE"
# ✉️ Email the kubeconfig
EMAIL_BODY_FILE=$(mktemp --suffix=.html)
cat <<EOF > "$EMAIL_BODY_FILE"
<html>
  <body style="font-family: Arial, sans-serif; color: #333;">
    <h2 style="color:#2F5496;">Kubernetes Cluster Access Details</h2>
    <p>Dear <strong>$USER</strong>,</p>
    <p>You have been granted access to the Kubernetes namespace <strong>$NAMESPACE</strong> on the cluster <strong>$CLUSTER_NAME</strong>.</p>
    <h3>Important Details:</h3>
    <ul>
      <li><strong>API Server URL:</strong> <code>$CLUSTER_SERVER</code></li>
      <li><strong>Namespace:</strong> $NAMESPACE</li>
      <li><strong>User:</strong> $USER</li>
      <li><strong>Role:</strong> Developer (Permissions to create, update, delete, and view resources like pods, deployments, jobs, services, configmaps, and secrets)</li>
    <h3>Using Your Kubeconfig</h3>
    <p>The attached <code>$USER.kubeconfig</code> file contains all the necessary credentials and cluster info to access Kubernetes resources.</p>
    <p>Please save the file securely on your local machine. To use it, run the following command in your terminal:</p>
    <pre style="background:#f4f4f4; padding:10px; border-radius:4px;">export KUBECONFIG=\$HOME/$USER.kubeconfig</pre>
    <p>After setting the environment variable, test your access by running:</p>
    <pre style="background:#f4f4f4; padding:10px; border-radius:4px;">kubectl get pods</pre>    <p>
      <strong>Please keep your kubeconfig file confidential.</strong> It contains credentials that grant access to the cluster.<br>
      Do not share this file via unsecured channels.<br>
      If you suspect your kubeconfig has been compromised, contact the cluster administrator immediately.
    </p>
    <p>If you have any questions or need further assistance, feel free to reach out.</p>
    <p>Best regards,<br>The Kubernetes Admin Team</p></html>
echo -e "${GREEN}📧 Sending email to ${EMAIL} using mutt...${NC}"
echo "Please see the attached kubeconfig file." | mutt -e "set content_type=text/html" -s "Kubernetes Access for $USER" -a "$KUBECONFIG_FILE" -- "$EMAIL" < "$EMAIL_BODY_FILE"
echo -e "${GREEN}✅ User '$USER' created and kubeconfig sent to $EMAIL.${NC}"

1. Shebang and Strict Mode

#!/bin/bashset -euo pipefail
  • #!/bin/bash: Specifies the script should run with the Bash shell.
  • -e: Exit immediately if any command returns a non-zero status.
  • -u: Treat unset variables as an error and exit immediately.
  • -o pipefail: Causes a pipeline to fail if any command within it fails, not just the last one.

This ensures the script stops on errors and avoids silent failures or undefined variables.

2. Comments for Readability

#----------------------------## 🚀 Kubernetes User Creator #
#----------------------------#

A header block showing the script’s purpose — creating Kubernetes users.

USER="$1"NAMESPACE="$2"

The script expects three command-line arguments:

  • $1: Username for the new Kubernetes user.
  • $2: Kubernetes namespace where the user will have access.
  • $3: Email to send the kubeconfig file.
KIND_CONTAINER="multi-node-cluster-control-plane"OUTPUT_DIR="./$USER"
  • KIND_CONTAINER: The Docker container name running the Kind Kubernetes control plane.
  • OUTPUT_DIR: Directory to save generated files for this user, named after the username.

5. Terminal Colors for Output

GREEN='\033[0;32m'RED='\033[0;31m'YELLOW='\033[1;33m'

Defines escape sequences for colorful terminal messages for better readability.

if [[ -z "$USER" || -z "$NAMESPACE" || -z "$EMAIL" ]]; then  echo -e "${RED}❗ Usage: $0 <username> <namespace> <email>${NC}"fi

Checks if any required argument is empty (-z means zero length). If yes, it prints usage info and exits.

7. Starting Message & Directory Setup

echo -e "${BLUE}👤 Creating user '${YELLOW}$USER${BLUE}' in namespace '${YELLOW}$NAMESPACE${BLUE}'...${NC}"mkdir -p "$OUTPUT_DIR"
  • Prints a user-friendly message.
  • Creates the output directory (mkdir -p creates parent directories as needed).

8. Verify Kind Cluster Running

if ! docker ps --format '{{.Names}}' | grep -q "^${KIND_CONTAINER}$"; then  echo -e "${RED}❌ Kind container '$KIND_CONTAINER' is not running!${NC}"fi
  • Uses docker ps to list running containers.
  • --format '{{.Names}}' lists container names only.
  • Checks if the Kind container is running.
  • If not, prints an error and exits.

9. Generate User’s Private Key and CSR

echo -e "${GREEN}🔑 Generating key and CSR for ${USER}...${NC}"openssl genrsa -out "$OUTPUT_DIR/$USER.key" 2048
openssl req -new -key "$OUTPUT_DIR/$USER.key" -out "$OUTPUT_DIR/$USER.csr" -subj "/CN=$USER/O=$USER"
  • openssl genrsa creates a 2048-bit private RSA key.
  • openssl req -new creates a Certificate Signing Request (CSR) using the key.
  • The CSR includes the subject with Common Name (CN) and Organization (O) set to username.

10. Sign CSR Using Kind’s Cluster CA

echo -e "${GREEN}📜 Signing CSR inside Kind container...${NC}"docker cp "$OUTPUT_DIR/$USER.csr" "$KIND_CONTAINER:/var/tmp/$USER.csr"
docker exec "$KIND_CONTAINER" openssl x509 -req -in "/var/tmp/$USER.csr" \
  -CA /etc/kubernetes/pki/ca.crt \
  -CAkey /etc/kubernetes/pki/ca.key \
  -CAcreateserial \
  -out "/var/tmp/$USER.crt" -days 365
docker cp "$KIND_CONTAINER:/var/tmp/$USER.crt" "$OUTPUT_DIR/$USER.crt"
  • Copies the CSR file into the Kind container.
  • Inside the container, signs the CSR with the Kubernetes cluster CA (Certificate Authority), creating a signed client certificate valid for 365 days.
  • Copies the signed certificate back out to the host machine.

11. Build kubeconfig File for the User

echo -e "${GREEN}⚙️  Building kubeconfig for ${USER}...${NC}"CLUSTER_NAME=$(kubectl config view --minify -o jsonpath='{.clusters[0].name}')
CLUSTER_SERVER=$(kubectl config view --minify -o jsonpath='{.clusters[0].cluster.server}')
kubectl config view --raw --minify -o jsonpath='{.clusters[0].cluster.certificate-authority-data}' | base64 -d > "$OUTPUT_DIR/ca.crt"
KUBECONFIG_FILE="$OUTPUT_DIR/$USER.kubeconfig"
  • Extracts the cluster name and API server URL from the current kubeconfig (usually ~/.kube/config).
  • Extracts and decodes the cluster CA cert and saves it locally.
  • Prepares the path for the user’s kubeconfig file.

12. Set Cluster in kubeconfig

kubectl config --kubeconfig="$KUBECONFIG_FILE" set-cluster "$CLUSTER_NAME" \  --server="$CLUSTER_SERVER" \
  --certificate-authority="$OUTPUT_DIR/ca.crt" \
  --embed-certs=true
  • Adds cluster info (server URL and CA certificate) to the user’s kubeconfig.
  • --embed-certs=true embeds the cert directly in kubeconfig.

13. Set User Credentials in kubeconfig

kubectl config --kubeconfig="$KUBECONFIG_FILE" set-credentials "$USER" \  --client-certificate="$OUTPUT_DIR/$USER.crt" \
  --client-key="$OUTPUT_DIR/$USER.key" \
  --embed-certs=true
  • Adds the client certificate and private key (generated and signed earlier) for authentication.
  • Certificates are embedded inline.

14. Set Context in kubeconfig

kubectl config --kubeconfig="$KUBECONFIG_FILE" set-context "$USER-context" \  --cluster="$CLUSTER_NAME" \  --namespace="$NAMESPACE"
kubectl config --kubeconfig="$KUBECONFIG_FILE" use-context "$USER-context"
  • Defines a context tying together the cluster, user, and namespace.
  • Sets this context as the default for the kubeconfig.

15. Create RBAC Role and RoleBinding

echo -e "${GREEN}🔐 Creating RBAC roles and bindings...${NC}"cat <<EOF | kubectl apply -f -
apiVersion: rbac.authorization.k8s.io/v1
kind: Role  namespace: $NAMESPACErules:
- apiGroups: ["", "apps", "batch"]
  resources: ["pods", "deployments", "jobs", "services", "configmaps", "secrets"]
  verbs: ["get", "watch", "list", "create", "update", "patch", "delete"]
EOF
kubectl create rolebinding "${USER}-dev-binding" \
  --namespace="$NAMESPACE" \
  --role="${USER}-dev-role" \
  --user="$USER" \
  --dry-run=client -o yaml | kubectl apply -f -
  • Creates a Role scoped to the namespace allowing full CRUD on common resource types.
  • Creates a RoleBinding to bind the role to the user.

openssl genrsa -out … openssl req -new …

- `openssl genrsa`: Creates a 2048-bit private key.- `openssl req -new`: Generates a Certificate Signing Request (CSR) with CN and O set to the username.### 10. Sign CSR with Kubernetes CA (inside Kind)docker cp ...
  • Copies the CSR to the Kind container.
  • Uses openssl x509 -req inside the container to sign the CSR using the Kubernetes CA files (ca.crt and ca.key).
  • Retrieves the signed certificate.

11. Build Kubeconfig for the User

CLUSTER_NAME=$(kubectl config view ...)kubectl config --kubeconfig=... set-cluster ...
  • Extracts cluster name and API server address.
  • Extracts the CA certificate and writes it to ca.crt.
  • Creates a kubeconfig that:
  • Adds user credentials (cert/key).
  • Sets a context using the namespace.

12. Create RBAC Role & RoleBinding

kubectl apply -f - <<EOF...
  • Creates a Role scoped to the namespace
  • Grants access to common development resources (pods, deployments, services, etc.).
  • Binds the role to the user using a RoleBinding.

13. Send Email with Kubeconfig

cat <<EOF > "$EMAIL_BODY_FILE"...
mutt -e "set content_type=text/html" ...
  • Generates an HTML email explaining how to use the kubeconfig.
  • Sends it with mutt, attaching the file and setting content type to HTML.
echo -e "${GREEN}✅ User '$USER' created and kubeconfig sent to $EMAIL.${NC}"
  • Prints a success message.

This script is a powerful tool for automating user access in Kubernetes. It reduces manual effort, increases security by generating isolated keys, and provides a professional user experience with email delivery.

items.title: API Gateway vs ESB 101
items.author: M Mahdi Ramadhan, M. Si
items.content:
In modern system architecture, particularly for enterprises and digital organizations, two key components are often used to manage and integrate services: and (also known as ESB — Enterprise Service Bus). Although both are involved in the system communication flow, their focus, purpose, and architecture differ significantly.

This article explores the deep differences between API Gateway and Integration Platform with real-world implementation examples combined with my experience: as the API Gateway and as the Integration Platform.

1. Definition and Purpose

An API Gateway is an architectural component that acts as a single entry point for all API requests from clients to backend services. Its main responsibilities include:

  • Routing requests to appropriate services
  • Authentication and authorization
  • Rate limiting & throttling
  • Logging & monitoring (can be integrated with monitoring platforms like Zipkin, Prometheus, Dynatrace, etc.)
  • Light transformation (headers, path, even body adjustments like Kong’s JSON transformer plugin)

Integration Platform (ESB)

An Enterprise Service Bus (ESB) is an architectural model used within integration platforms that features complex message routing capabilities. It can intelligently forward messages to various systems or services, possibly changing protocols or formats based on conditions like message content or destination. This makes it ideal for intricate integration scenarios involving multiple systems and business rules.

An Integration Platform is middleware that connects various applications and services across technologies and protocols. Its core focus:

  • Integration across heterogeneous systems (SAP, database, file, API)
  • Business workflow orchestration
  • Complex data transformation (EDI, XML, JSON, CSV)
  • Workflow logic and conditional routing

Architecture with Kong / Apigee

  • Suitable for API exposure
  • Focus on API rate-limiting, security, observability, integration between microservice with request and response transformer

Architecture with WebMethods / MuleSoft

  • Workflow orchestration between services
  • Transport protocol conversion
  • Message format transformation
  • Business service abstraction (decoupling)
  • Transaction management and security enforcement

Use API Gateway (Kong, Apigee) if:

  • You focus on exposing APIs to frontend/public
  • Need traffic management and throttling
  • Require OAuth2 / JWT-based authentication
  • Following API-first architecture

Use Integration Platform (WebMethods, MuleSoft) if:

  • Integrating legacy and modern systems
  • Need workflow, data transformation, and retries
  • Working with EDI, HL7, SAP, JMS
  • Require complex business orchestration

Combining API Gateway and Integration Platform is highly recommended for modern enterprise architecture. Combine when:

  • The system must expose external APIs for frontend or partners, but also requires backend orchestration and integration
  • You need to separate responsibilities between API access management and backend integration logic
  • You’re undergoing digital transformation or legacy modernization while maintaining complex integration and secure API exposure

When ESB Becomes Overkill

While Integration Platforms like WebMethods and MuleSoft is not open source and provide rich orchestration and transformation features, there are scenarios where using a full ESB stack may be excessive — both in cost and complexity. Examples include:

  • Simple Event Streaming or Message Queuing Needs: When the use case only involves passing data between two or more systems asynchronously without transformation or workflow (e.g., IoT sensor messages, log shipping), tools like , , or are more lightweight and cost-effective.
  • Stateless and API-First Architecture: In microservices architectures where stateless communication and simple protocol mediation suffice, an API Gateway coupled with a message broker or FaaS (Function as a Service) model may eliminate the need for a heavyweight ESB.
  • Cloud-Native Applications: When services are already containerized and connected via service meshes like Istio or Linkerd, relying on an ESB for protocol mediation or transformation can be redundant.

API Gateway and Integration Platform are not exclusive choices — they are two sides of the same coin in modern integration architecture. API Gateways like Kong and Apigee excel in service , , and via APIs, while Integration Platforms like WebMethods and MuleSoft play a crucial role in data transformation, cross-system orchestration, and legacy system integration. Organizations that succeed in digital transformation are those capable of adopting , combining API-first with system-of-record integration.

Think of API Gateway as the doorman, and Integration Platform as the butler orchestrating everything behind the scenes.

API Gateway vs ESB 101 was originally published in DevOps.dev on Medium, where people are continuing the conversation by highlighting and responding to this story.

items.title: Dockerizing Monitoring Tools
items.author: Mahmoud Khatib
items.content:
Part 4: Monitoring Network Devices Using Grafana and Zabbix

This is Part 4 of the “Dockerizing Monitoring Tool” series, where we’ve been working on setting up a multi-container Docker Compose microservice infrastructure. This setup includes containers running various monitoring services.

In , we launched an EC2 instance and used Docker Compose to run Grafana in a container, ready to receive data from multiple sources.

In , we deployed a second EC2 instance within the same VPC subnet to host the service, which serves as a data target for Prometheus, collecting OS and hardware metrics from the EC2 instances.

In , we deployed to monitor services at the container level.

In this article (), we will focus on monitoring using . This enables us to monitor any network device that supports the . Zabbix will run on a dedicated server (called “Monitoring Server”), collect metrics from configured SNMP agents, and serve this data to as a data source. Finally, we’ll import dashboards into Grafana to visualize the collected network metrics.

In previous articles, we integrated Grafana with Prometheus as the primary data source to collect metrics from various endpoints, including Windows, Linux, and Docker services. Grafana was used to pull this data from Prometheus and visualize it through dashboards. In this article, I’m adding Zabbix as an additional data source, specifically to collect metrics from network devices that support the SNMP protocol.

What is Zabbix? Zabbix is an enterprise-grade open-source monitoring solution. Its architecture is composed of several key components that work together to collect, store, and analyze monitoring data.

Let’s walk through the components of Zabbix and explain how they work together:

2–1- Zabbix Application Stack

Zabbix application stack consists of multiple components, which includes:

  1. This is the central component of Zabbix. It performs the polling and trapping of data, calculates triggers, sends notifications, and stores all configuration, statistical, and operational data. It communicates with Zabbix agents, Zabbix database and the web interface2
  2. All configuration information and collected data are stored in a database. Zabbix supports various databases, including MySQL, PostgreSQL, Oracle, MariaDB. While technically a separate component, it’s essential for Zabbix Server to function.
  3. Zabbix Web Interface (Frontend): A web-based interface provides an easy way to access and manage Zabbix from any platform. It’s typically deployed on a web server (like Apache or Nginx) with PHP support. It communicates with the Zabbix Server to display data and manage configurations.

2–2 Internal Zabbix Component Communication Flow

In the below diagram, I am breaking down the main components and its relationship with the other components and their default port numbers:

When discussing Zabbix traffic flow, it’s helpful to categorize it into two main sections:

  • Zabbix Server and Database (DB): The Zabbix server stores all collected monitoring data and configuration in a database. It connects to the database using the database’s default port. For MySQL/MariaDB, this is typically , and for PostgreSQL, it’s .
  • Zabbix Server and Frontend: The Zabbix frontend is a web-based interface that users interact with. It runs on a web server (like Apache or Nginx) and communicates with the Zabbix server to retrieve and display monitoring data. The frontend connects to the Zabbix server, usually on , which is Zabbix’s “trapper” port for receiving data. The frontend itself is accessed via standard web ports: (for HTTP) or (for HTTPS).

It is also worth mentioning the optional components:

  1. These are deployed on the monitored targets (servers, virtual machines, etc.).
  • They actively monitor local resources and applications (e.g., CPU, memory, disk usage, services, log files) and report the gathered data to the Zabbix Server or Zabbix Proxy.

Agents can operate in two modes:

  • The Zabbix Server or Proxy initiates a connection to the agent to request data.
  • The Zabbix agent periodically asks the Zabbix Server or Proxy for a list of items to collect and then sends the collected data back.

2–3- How Zabbix works with Grafana

Zabbix and Grafana work together by Zabbix acting as the data collector and storage backend, while Grafana serves as the visualization and dashboarding frontend. The Zabbix application stack that consist of Zabbix server, Zabbix database (Postgre SQL), and Zabbix frontend/web interface (PHP-based application), while Grafana consists of frontend and backend MySQL lite database.

Let is draw the below diagram to explain the integration between Zabbix and Grafana in more detail:

Below, we’ll walk through the steps outlined above to explain the communication flow between Zabbix and Grafana:

0- Zabbix collects and stores monitoring data in its database.

1- Grafana sends queries to the Zabbix frontend service (via the Zabbix API), requesting specific historical or real-time metrics such as CPU utilization or disk usage.

2- The Zabbix frontend works with the Zabbix server and the database to process these API requests.

3- The Zabbix frontend retrieves the requested data from the database.

4- The Zabbix frontend sends the data back to Grafana.

5- Grafana visualizes the data, rendering it into interactive graphs, charts, and dashboards for easier analysis.

Note: The Zabbix web frontend and Zabbix server communicate primarily through the

2–3- Why I am used Zabbix as a data source/collector:

Initially, I tried using Prometheus for collecting network data. While it worked, I encountered several challenges during configuration and ongoing management. Below, I’ll summarize the main reasons why I chose to use Zabbix instead of Prometheus for network data collection:

  • Prometheus typically “pulls” metrics from HTTP endpoints.
  • It doesn’t natively speak SNMP. To monitor SNMP devices with Prometheus, you need an . This is an additional component that acts as a proxy, polling SNMP devices and then exposing those metrics to Prometheus via an HTTP endpoint. This adds an extra layer of complexity and a potential point of failure.
  • Less “Out-of-the-Box” for Networks: While it can monitor network devices via the SNMP Exporter, the tooling for network-specific features like trap processing, comprehensive templates, and network maps isn’t as integrated or mature as it is in Zabbix.

The high-level design outlined in this article provides an overview of our solution setup. It includes an on-premises monitoring server, which in this case runs Linux Ubuntu 24.04, and an on-premises firewall located on the same LAN network (10.0.0.0/16). We will deploy a monitoring stack consisting of Zabbix and Grafana on the monitoring server using Docker Compose (bridge network 172.18.0.0/16). Zabbix will serve as the data collector, while Grafana will be used for visualization. The two targets firewall & NAS storge will function as an SNMP agent. Our objective is to monitor the firewall & the storge via the SNMP protocol and transmit the collected data metrics to Grafana for analysis and visualization.

Our solution presented in this article is part of a series I began last year. The goal of this installment is to enable monitoring of various network devices — such as switches, firewalls, NAS storage, or any device that supports the SNMP protocol.

4- Overview of the tasks:

0- Enable and configure SNMP V2 protocol on the on prem firewall.

1- Configure the Docker compose yaml file that contains multiple containers for Zabbix and Grafana.

2- Import the necessary SNMP MIB into Zabbix.

3- Add Zabbix as a data source to Grafana.

4- Add Zabbix plugin to Grafana and enable it.

5- Configure the Grafana dashboards to visuals the collected data metrics from the firewall.

4–1 Step-by-step Instructions:

4–0- Access the on prem server via ssh, and create a new the YAML file by typing the following command:

sudo nano docker-compose.yml

Then, add the below code into the docker compose yaml file:

version: "3.8"  zabbix-server:
    image: ${ZABBIX_SERVER_IMAGE:-zabbix/zabbix-server-pgsql:ubuntu-7.2-latest}
    container_name: server    ports:    environment:      DB_SERVER_PORT: 5432
      POSTGRES_USER: ${POSTGRES_USER:-zabbix}
      POSTGRES_PASSWORD: ${POSTGRES_PASSWORD:-zabbix}
      POSTGRES_DB: ${POSTGRES_DB:-zabbix}
    depends_on:    networks:    image: ${ZABBIX_FRONTEND_IMAGE:-zabbix/zabbix-web-nginx-pgsql:ubuntu-7.2-latest}    restart: unless-stopped      - "8080:8080"    environment:      POSTGRES_USER: ${POSTGRES_USER:-zabbix}
      POSTGRES_PASSWORD: ${POSTGRES_PASSWORD:-zabbix}
      POSTGRES_DB: ${POSTGRES_DB:-zabbix}      ZBX_SERVER_HOST: zabbix-server    depends_on:    networks:    image: ${ZABBIX_AGENT_IMAGE:-zabbix/zabbix-agent:ubuntu-7.2-latest}
    container_name: zabbix-agent
    restart: unless-stoppez      - "10050:10050"      ZBX_ACTIVE_ALLOW: false      ZBX_SERVER_HOST: zabbix-server      ZBX_HOSTNAME: zabbix-agent
      ZBX_HOSTNAMEITEM: system.hostname
    depends_on:    networks:    image: ${GRAFANA_IMAGE:-grafana/grafana}    restart: unless-stopped      - "3000:3000"      GF_SECURITY_ADMIN_USER: ${GRAFANA_USER:-admin}
      GF_SECURITY_ADMIN_PASSWORD: ${GRAFANA_SECRET:-12345}
      GF_INSTALL_PLUGINS: alexanderzobnin-zabbix-app    user: "472"      - grafana:/var/lib/grafana
      - ./grafana/grafana.ini:/etc/grafana/grafana.ini
      - ./grafana/provisioning:/etc/grafana/provisioning      - zabbix-frontend      - network-zabbiz

Let is bring up and run services defined in a docker-compose.yml using the following command:

Below will see that all containers are running:

4–2- Access Zabbix dashboard: The Zabbix application stack consists of three main services or Docker containers, as previously described. In this guide, we will focus on accessing the Zabbix frontend web interface. To do this, open any web browser and enter the monitoring server’s IP address followed by port 8080 (e.g., http://<server-ip>:8080). This will take you to the Zabbix web interface, as shown below:

On the dashboard, you’ll notice a default entry labeled , which represents the monitoring server itself. This appears because, as you may recall, we installed the Zabbix agent on the same server that is running the Zabbix server.

Before adding a Fortinet firewall to Zabbix for monitoring, we will import the Fortinet template into Zabbix, to find the template visit and search []. Below screenshot shows how to create the template, we will name the template “Network”

4–4- Add the template (MIB files): Within Data Collector section will import the Fortinet template into the Network template we create above as below:

Within Data Collector section will add a new host group and will name “Firewalls” as the below screenshot shows:

Next Within Data Collector section, we’ll create our first host in Zabbix — the Fortinet firewall — as shown in the screenshot below. Start by giving the host a meaningful name and assigning it to the host group we created earlier. Then, configure the SNMP interface by providing the IP address or DNS name of the firewall, selecting the appropriate SNMP version, and entering the SNMP community string.

4–7- Let is go to Grafana and add Zabbix as a new data source:

You need to go to [6] to download the right template for your case.

4–8- Let is create a new Grafana panel and add the target (NAS storage).

The screenshot below illustrates the steps to add NAS storage monitoring by selecting Zabbix as the data source. From there, you can select the NAS device and configure the relevant SNMP MIB parameters and metrics. For our use case, we will focus on selecting the ICMP ping metric to monitor device availability.

After saving the configuration, we go to the main dashboard page and see the panel:

I will mention below some commands that helped me troubleshoot the issues during the deployments:

5–1- See docker ID and name:

docker ps - format "table {{.ID}}\t{{.Names}}

5–2- Stop and remove all containers (if needed, this in the case when you stop the containers and delete):

sudo docker rm -f $(sudo docker ps -aq)

5–3- Delete all custom bridge networks:

sudo docker network prune -f

5–4- To Delete All Volumes (including ones shown):

sudo docker volume rm $(sudo docker volume ls -q)sudo docker volume ls

5–5- Show docker logs (example, Zabbix-server):

docker compose logs zabbix-server

5–6- Stop and disable the Linux service itself (example, zabbix-agent):

sudo systemctl stop zabbix-agentsudo systemctl disable zabbix-agent

5–7- Firewall rules need to be configured to allow communication on these ports between the different Zabbix components.

5–8- Before adding a Fortinet firewall (or any network device) to Zabbix for monitoring, you’ll generally need to import specific Zabbix designed for that device. Zabbix doesn’t have a single “universal” template that works perfectly for every network device because they all expose different metrics and use different MIBs (Management Information Bases) for SNMP.

By integrating Zabbix into our Docker-based monitoring stack, we’ve extended our observability capabilities beyond system and container-level metrics to include network devices via SNMP. This addition complements the existing setup with Grafana, Prometheus, Node Exporter, and cAdvisor, creating a unified and scalable monitoring solution across hybrid environments. With Grafana visualizing metrics from both infrastructure and network layers, we now have a comprehensive view of system health, performance, and availability — laying the groundwork for proactive operations and smarter troubleshooting.

Next, we’ll write a theory article to wrap up all four parts of the series, providing an overview of the monitoring solution we’ve built using Grafana, Prometheus, cAdvisor, and Zabbix to monitor our infrastructure.

items.title: Stopped Docker Compose, But Services Still Running? Here’s How I Fixed This Strange Issue.
items.author: Taha Yousuf Ali
items.content:
Recently, I ran into a strange issue while working on our microservices-based application running inside Docker containers in our Dev environment. Even after stopping some containers (and even bringing down the entire Docker Compose setup), I found the services still accessible. At first, I was puzzled. If containers are down, how are the services still alive?

It turned into a deep debugging session, and I want to share what I found and how I fixed it — so you don’t have to go through the same pain!

◙ → Some containers were stopped using docker-compose down. ◙ → Yet, their services were still reachable via the network.

◙ → I ran ps aux | grep <app> and found that the app processes were still running.

◙ → Even after killing the parent processes and restarting the VM, these processes came back automatically as soon as Docker started — even though docker-compose wasn’t reapplied!

This unexpected behavior also caused failures in our Hyperledger Fabric-based system. Our orderer component became corrupted — but I couldn’t even begin to fix that until I handled these phantom processes.

When containers are stopped, Docker is supposed to terminate the processes running inside them. However, in some rare cases, leftover process states can occur due to:

◙ → Docker daemon bugs or crashes.◙ → Incorrect shutdowns (e.g., forced reboot while containers are running).

◙ → Corruption in Docker’s metadata or storage layer (overlay2).

◙ → Auto-start flags or leftover state from container restore mechanisms.

In my case, the processes were likely being restarted because Docker had preserved container metadata and overlay layers even after docker-compose down. Docker may have marked these containers for auto-restart based on previously defined flags or leftover state.

Here are the exact steps I used to resolve the issue:

1. Clean Up All Unused Docker Objects

docker system prune -a 
#be patient, above command might take some time to execute :D

This removes all stopped containers, networks not used by at least one container, dangling images, and build cache.

2. Remove Residual Docker State

After pruning, I checked the Docker state directory:

sudo ls -l /var/lib/docker/

I still saw old containers/ and overlay2/ directories — which shouldn't exist after a full prune.

So I took the nuclear option (⚠️ do this only if you’re okay wiping all Docker state):

sudo systemctl stop docker
sudo rm -rf /var/lib/docker/containers/*
sudo rm -rf /var/lib/docker/overlay2/*
sudo systemctl start docker

This wiped out all Docker images, containers, volumes, and overlay metadata — resetting Docker to a clean slate.

🛠 What Happened Behind the Scenes?

Let me explain this in layman terms:

Docker stores container state and filesystem layers in its internal storage directory: /var/lib/docker/. Normally, docker-compose down cleans up what's needed. But when Docker doesn’t shut down gracefully or if the daemon bugs out, it can leave behind orphaned data — like zombie containers or filesystem overlays.

◙ → Even though you think containers are gone, Docker still knows about them.◙ → And if those containers were set to auto-restart, Docker will restart them as soon as its service comes up — even without a Compose file in play.

◙ → The stale processes were finally gone.◙ → Docker started cleanly with no phantom containers.

◙ → I could finally focus on fixing the Hyperledger Fabric orderer corruption — which I’ll cover in another post maybe 😉.

This kind of issue is tricky because you don’t expect processes to live beyond their containers. If you’re seeing ghost processes or mystery services after stopping Docker — don’t forget to check the underlying Docker storage.

: If you’re in production, never do rm -rf /var/lib/docker/ without a full backup and stakeholder approval!

If this helped, share it with your team — or save it for a rainy day when your containers go rogue.

Khitaaaaam….THANK YOU !!!

items.title: Docker Desktop 4.42: Native IPv6, Built-In MCP, and Better Model Packaging
items.author: Deanna Sparks
items.content:
Docker Desktop 4.42 introduces powerful new capabilities that enhance network flexibility, improve security, and deepen AI toolchain integration, all while reducing setup friction. With native IPv6 support, a fully integrated MCP Toolkit, and major upgrades to Docker Model Runner and our AI agent Gordon, this release continues our commitment to helping developers move faster, ship smarter, and build securely across any environment. Whether you’re managing enterprise-grade networks or experimenting with agentic workflows, Docker Desktop 4.42 brings the tools you need right into your development workflows. 

Docker Desktop now provides IPv6 networking capabilities with customization options to better support diverse network environments. You can now choose between dual IPv4/IPv6 (default), IPv4-only, or IPv6-only networking modes to align with your organization’s network requirements. The new intelligent DNS resolution behavior automatically detects your host’s network stack and filters unsupported record types, preventing connectivity timeouts in IPv4-only or IPv6-only environments. 

These ipv6 settings are available in Docker Desktop Settings > Resources > Network section and can be enforced across teams using Settings Management, making Docker Desktop more reliable in complex enterprise network configurations including IPv6-only deployments.

Figure 1: Docker Desktop IPv6 settings

Docker MCP Toolkit integrated into Docker Desktop

Last month, we launched the Docker MCP Catalog and Toolkit to help developers easily discover MCP servers and securely connect them to their favorite clients and agentic apps. We’re humbled by the incredible support from the community. User growth is up by over 50%, and we’ve crossed 1 million pulls! Now, we’re excited to share that the MCP Toolkit is built right into Docker Desktop, no separate extension required.

You can now access more than 100 MCP servers, including GitHub, MongoDB, Hashicorp, and more, directly from Docker Desktop – just enable the servers you need, configure them, and connect to clients like Claude Desktop, Cursor, Continue.dev, or Docker’s AI agent Gordon.

Unlike typical setups that run MCP servers via npx or uvx processes with broad access to the host system, Docker Desktop runs these servers inside isolated containers with well-defined security boundaries. All container images are cryptographically signed, with proper isolation of secrets and configuration data. 

Figure 2: Docker MCP Toolkit is now integrated natively into Docker Desktop

To meet developers where they are, we’re bringing Docker MCP support to the CLI, using the same command structure you’re already familiar with. With the new docker mcp commands, you can launch, configure, and manage MCP servers directly from the terminal. The CLI plugin offers comprehensive functionality, including catalog management, client connection setup, and secret management.

Figure 3:  Docker MCP CLI commands.

Docker AI Agent Gordon Now Supports MCP Toolkit Integration

In this release, we’ve upgraded Gordon, Docker’s AI agent, with direct integration to the MCP Toolkit in Docker Desktop. To enable it, open Gordon, click the “Tools” button, and toggle on the “MCP” Toolkit option. Once activated, the MCP Toolkit tab will display tools available from any MCP servers you’ve configured.

Figure 4: Docker’s AI Agent Gordon now integrates with Docker’s MCP Toolkit, bringing 100+ MCP servers

This integration gives you immediate access to 100+ MCP servers with no extra setup, letting you experiment with AI capabilities directly in your Docker workflow. Gordon now acts as a bridge between Docker’s native tooling and the broader AI ecosystem, letting you leverage specialized tools for everything from screenshot capture to data analysis and API interactions – all from a consistent, unified interface.

Figure 5: Docker’s AI Agent Gordon uses the GitHub MCP server to pull issues and suggest solutions.

Finally, we’ve also improved the Dockerize feature with expanded support for Java, Kotlin, Gradle, and Maven projects. These improvements make it easier to containerize a wider range of applications with minimal configuration. With expanded containerization capabilities and integrated access to the MCP Toolkit, Gordon is more powerful than ever. It streamlines container workflows, reduces repetitive tasks, and gives you access to specialized tools, so you can stay focused on building, shipping, and running your applications efficiently.

Docker Model Runner adds Qualcomm support, Docker Engine Integration, and UX Upgrades

Staying true to our philosophy of giving developers more flexibility and meeting them where they are, the latest version of Docker Model Runner adds broader OS support, deeper integration with popular Docker tools, and improvements in both performance and usability.

In addition to supporting Apple Silicon and Windows systems with NVIDIA GPUs, Docker Model Runner now works on Windows devices with Qualcomm chipsets. Under the hood, we’ve upgraded our inference engine to use the latest version of llama.cpp, bringing significantly enhanced tool calling capabilities to your AI applications.Docker Model Runner can now be installed directly in Docker Engine Community Edition across multiple Linux distributions supported by Docker Engine. This integration is particularly valuable for developers looking to incorporate AI capabilities into their CI/CD pipelines and automated testing workflows. To get started, check out our documentation for thesetup guide.

Get Up and Running with Models Faster

The Docker Model Runner user experience has been upgraded with expanded GUI functionality in Docker Desktop. All of these UI enhancements are designed to help you get started with Model Runner quickly and build applications faster. A dedicated interface now includes three new tabs that simplify model discovery, management, and streamline troubleshooting workflows. Additionally, Docker Desktop’s updated GUI introduces a more intuitive onboarding experience with streamlined “two-click” actions.

After clicking on the Model tab, you’ll see three new sub-tabs. The first, labeled “Local,” displays a set of models in various sizes that you can quickly pull. Once a model is pulled, you can launch a chat interface to test and experiment with it immediately.

Figure 6: Access a set of models of various sizes to get quickly started in Models menu of Docker Desktop

The second tab ”Docker Hub” offers a comprehensive view for browsing and pulling models from Docker Hub’s AI Catalog, making it easy to get started directly within Docker Desktop, without switching contexts.

Figure 7: A shortcut to the Model catalog from Docker Hub in Models menu of Docker Desktop

The third tab “Logs” offers real-time access to the inference engine’s log tail, giving developers immediate visibility into model execution status and debugging information directly within the Docker Desktop interface.

Figure 8: Gain visibility into model execution status and debugging information in Docker Desktop

Model Packaging Made Simple via CLI

As part of the Docker Model CLI, the most significant enhancement is the introduction of the docker model package command. This new command enables developers to package their models from GGUF format into OCI-compliant artifacts, fundamentally transforming how AI models are distributed and shared. It enables seamless publishing to both public and private and OCI-compatible repositories such as Docker Hub and establishes a standardized, secure workflow for model distribution, using the same trusted Docker tools developers already rely on. See our docs for more details. 

From intelligent networking enhancements to seamless AI integrations, Docker Desktop 4.42 makes it easier than ever to build with confidence. With native support for IPv6, in-app access to 100+ MCP servers, and expanded platform compatibility for Docker Model Runner, this release is all about meeting developers where they are and equipping them with the tools to take their work further. Update to the latest version today and unlock everything Docker Desktop 4.42 has to offer.

items.title: Enhancing Kubernetes Event Management with Custom Aggregation
items.content:
Kubernetes Events provide crucial insights into cluster operations, but as clusters grow, managing and analyzing these events becomes increasingly challenging. This blog post explores how to build custom event aggregation systems that help engineering teams better understand cluster behavior and troubleshoot issues more effectively.

The challenge with Kubernetes events

In a Kubernetes cluster, events are generated for various operations - from pod scheduling and container starts to volume mounts and network configurations. While these events are invaluable for debugging and monitoring, several challenges emerge in production environments:

  1. : Large clusters can generate thousands of events per minute
  2. : Default event retention is limited to one hour
  3. : Related events from different components are not automatically linked
  4. : Events lack standardized severity or category classifications
  5. : Similar events are not automatically grouped

To learn more about Events in Kubernetes, read the Event API reference.

Consider a production environment with tens of microservices where the users report intermittent transaction failures:

Traditional event aggregation process: Engineers are wasting hours sifting through thousands of standalone events spread across namespaces. By the time they look into it, the older events have long since purged, and correlating pod restarts to node-level issues is practically impossible.

With its event aggregation in its custom events: The system groups events across resources, instantly surfacing correlation patterns such as volume mount timeouts before pod restarts. History indicates it occurred during past record traffic spikes, highlighting a storage scalability issue in minutes rather than hours.

The beneﬁt of this approach is that organizations that implement it commonly cut down their troubleshooting time significantly along with increasing the reliability of systems by detecting patterns early.

Building an Event aggregation system

This post explores how to build a custom event aggregation system that addresses these challenges, aligned to Kubernetes best practices. I've picked the Go programming language for my example.

This event aggregation system consists of three main components:

  1. : Monitors the Kubernetes API for new events
  2. : Processes, categorizes, and correlates events
  3. : Stores processed events for longer retention

Here's a sketch for how to implement the event watcher:

Event processing and classification

The event processor enriches events with additional context and classification:

Implementing Event correlation

One of the key features you could implement is a way of correlating related Events. Here's an example correlation strategy:

Event storage and retention

For long-term storage and analysis, you'll probably want a backend that supports:

  • Efficient querying of large event volumes
  • Flexible retention policies
  • Support for aggregation queries

Here's a sample storage interface:

Good practices for Event management

    • Implement rate limiting for event processing
    • Use efficient filtering at the API server level
    • Batch events for storage operations
    • Distribute event processing across multiple workers
    • Use leader election for coordination
    • Implement backoff strategies for API rate limits
    • Handle API server disconnections gracefully
    • Buffer events during storage backend unavailability
    • Implement retry mechanisms with exponential backoff

Implement pattern detection to identify recurring issues:

With this implementation, the system can identify recurring patterns such as node pressure events, pod scheduling failures, or networking issues that occur with a specific frequency.

The following example provides a starting point for building an alerting system based on event patterns. It is not a complete solution but a conceptual sketch to illustrate the approach.

A well-designed event aggregation system can significantly improve cluster observability and troubleshooting capabilities. By implementing custom event processing, correlation, and storage, operators can better understand cluster behavior and respond to issues more effectively.

The solutions presented here can be extended and customized based on specific requirements while maintaining compatibility with the Kubernetes API and following best practices for scalability and reliability.

Future enhancements could include:

  • Machine learning for anomaly detection
  • Integration with popular observability platforms
  • Custom event APIs for application-specific events
  • Enhanced visualization and reporting capabilities

items.title: AWS Weekly Roundup: New AWS Heroes, Amazon Q Developer, EC2 GPU price reduction, and more (June 9, 2025)
items.author: Betty Zheng (郑予彬)
items.content:
The AWS Heroes program recognizes a vibrant, worldwide group of AWS experts whose enthusiasm for knowledge-sharing has a real impact within the community. Heroes go above and beyond to share knowledge in a variety of ways in developer community. We introduce our newest AWS Heroes in the second quarter of 2025.

In addition to the inspiring celebrations, here are some AWS launches that caught my attention.

Here are some additional projects, blog posts that you might find interesting:

  • Introducing public AWS API models – AWS now provides daily updates of Smithy API models on GitHub, enabling developers to build custom SDK clients, understand AWS API behaviors, and create developer tools for better AWS service integration.
  • The AWS Asia Pacific (Taipei) Region is now open – The new Region provides customers with data residency requirements to securely store data in Taiwan while providing even lower latency. Customers across industries can benefit from the secure, scalable, and reliable cloud infrastructure to drive digital transformation and innovation.

Check your calendars and sign up for these upcoming AWS events.

  • Join re:Inforce from anywhere – If you aren’t able to make it to Philadelphia (June 16–18), tune in remotely. Get free access to the re:Inforce keynote and innovation talks live as they happen.
  • AWS Summits – Join free online and in-person events that bring the cloud computing community together to connect, collaborate, and learn about AWS. Register in your nearest city: Shanghai (June 19 – 20), Milano (June 18), Mumbai (June 19) and Japan (June 25 – 26).
  • AWS re:Invent – Mark your calendars for AWS re:Invent (December 1 – 5) in Las Vegas. Registration is now open
  • AWS Community Days – Join community-led conferences that feature technical discussions, workshops, and hands-on labs led by expert AWS users and industry leaders from around the world: Mexico (June 14), Nairobi, Kenya (June 14) and Colombia (June 28)

That’s all for this week. Check back next Monday for another Weekly Roundup!
